claim: An agentic loop fires once from a human, then the agent generates and reviews its own work before feeding back for iteration
actionable: Design agents with self-review capabilities so they can validate outputs against criteria before presenting to humans
source_title: WTF Is an "AI Agent Loop"? Genius or Hype?

claim: Human-in-the-loop agents maintain human direction and approval at each step, avoiding high token costs of wide-open loops
actionable: Keep humans in control by requiring approval or review before each major step rather than letting agents run unchecked
source_title: WTF Is an "AI Agent Loop"? Genius or Hype?

claim: Wide-open loops that run without checks can burn through $1.3 million in tokens per month at scale
actionable: Monitor agent loop token usage carefully and implement hard limits or human approval gates to prevent runaway costs
source_title: WTF Is an "AI Agent Loop"? Genius or Hype?

claim: Agentic loops are cost-efficient only with high-tier plans due to token consumption; lower tiers burn through quotas quickly
actionable: Reserve agentic loops for $200+/month plans; use human-in-the-loop for lower tiers to control costs
source_title: WTF Is an "AI Agent Loop"? Genius or Hype?

claim: Agentic loops work best for confined, binary-feedback tasks like code review where success is clearly measurable
actionable: Use agentic loops for well-scoped tasks with clear pass/fail criteria (code review, landing page SEO, etc.)
source_title: WTF Is an "AI Agent Loop"? Genius or Hype?

claim: A closed code-review loop using Cursor, GitHub, and Greptile can iteratively improve code to a 5/5 quality score
actionable: Connect Cursor to GitHub with Greptile for context, then loop: generate code, review against scoring criteria, iterate until 5/5
source_title: WTF Is an "AI Agent Loop"? Genius or Hype?
